metadata.source: Basic mushroom info expansion for master list

records.culinary_uses: Gelatinous edible mushroom typically used in soups, stir-fries, and mixed mushroom dishes for texture more than strong flavor.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly culinary, though related species are used in traditional food-medicine contexts.
records.notes: Brown to reddish-brown ear-shaped jelly fungus on dead hardwood, often after rain. Flexible, translucent to opaque, and rubbery when fresh.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when correctly identified and well cleaned. Best used for texture rather than expecting strong flavor.

records.culinary_uses: Small but good edible grassland mushroom often dried or used fresh in soups, sautés, and mixed dishes.
records.notes: Tan to buff lawn and meadow mushroom that often appears in arcs or rings. Tough stem, pale cap, widely spaced gills.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when correctly identified, but beginners need caution because several small lawn mushrooms are unsafe.

records.culinary_uses: Large cap mushroom valued for breading, frying, and slicing into substantial portions.
records.notes: Tall elegant mushroom with a snakeskin-patterned stem, broad cap scales, and a movable ring. Found in open grassy places and edges.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when correctly identified, but parasol-type mushrooms include problem species. Identification must be careful.

records.culinary_uses: Often eaten when young and firm, usually sautéed or breaded, but not tolerated by everyone.
records.notes: Large scaled parasol-type mushroom with a ring and stout stem, often in rich soil, composty ground, and edges.
records.edibility_detail: Edible for some people, but causes gastrointestinal upset in others. Treat as conditionally edible.

records.culinary_uses: Firm orange milkcap eaten sautéed, grilled, or pickled by experienced mushroom hunters.
records.notes: Orange mushroom with concentric zoning and orange latex when cut. Often associated with conifers.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when correctly identified. Milkcaps vary widely, so species-level confidence matters.

records.culinary_uses: Distinctive blue milkcap that can be cooked like other firm milkcaps when collected young and fresh.
records.notes: Blue to indigo mushroom with decurrent gills and blue latex when cut, fading with age.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when correctly identified, though quality varies with age and condition.

records.culinary_uses: Edible coral fungus often added to mixed mushroom dishes when young, fresh, and correctly identified.
records.notes: Whitish to pale yellow branching coral fungus on decaying hardwood, with crown-like tips at branch ends.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when fresh and correctly identified. Coral fungi as a group require caution.

records.culinary_uses: Edible jelly fungus, though usually a novelty or texture ingredient rather than a prized food mushroom.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly of curiosity or minor traditional interest rather than a major local food species.
records.notes: Bright yellow brainy jelly growth on dead hardwood, especially after wet weather.
records.edibility_detail: Technically edible, but usually gathered more as a curiosity than a staple food.

records.culinary_uses: Edible jelly fungus with little flavor, used more for texture or curiosity than as a serious harvest target.
records.medicinal_uses: Minimal food-medicine importance in this context.
records.notes: Orange to amber gelatinous clusters on decaying wood, especially in damp weather.
records.edibility_detail: Edible but not usually considered a major table mushroom.

records.culinary_uses: Small clustered puffball edible when the inside is solid white and fresh.
records.notes: Small tan to buff pear-shaped puffballs in clusters on rotting wood.
records.edibility_detail: Eat only when the interior is pure white with no darkening and no developing cap/gill structure.

records.culinary_uses: Edible puffball when young and white inside, usually sliced and sautéed or added to mixed dishes.
records.notes: Small pale puffball with spiny or warted surface when fresh, found on forest floor.
records.edibility_detail: Only edible while pure white inside. Discard once yellowing, browning, or softening begins.

records.culinary_uses: Unusual edible bracket sometimes sliced thin and cooked while young, valued more by enthusiasts than by casual foragers.
records.notes: Reddish fleshy bracket fungus with meat-like texture and moist cut surface, usually on hardwoods.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when young, but texture and acidity make it a niche mushroom for many people.

records.culinary_uses: Attractive red-and-yellow bolete eaten by experienced hunters when correctly identified and in good condition.
records.notes: Red cap tones, yellow pores, yellow stem with reddish blush, and blue bruising in some parts. Found on forest soil with hardwoods.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when correctly identified, but red-and-yellow boletes need care because some similar boletes are poor or unsafe choices.
